content.subtitle: Form
items: statement: I/you/we/they travel... He/she/it travels...
negative: I/you/we/they don't travel... He/she/it doesn't travel...
question: Do I/you/we/they travel...? Does he/she/it travel...?

content.subtitle: Use
items: Current habits: Toby walks to work.
To talk about how often things happen: Angela doesn't visit us very often.
Permanent situations: Carlo works in a travel agent's.
States: Do you have an up-to-date passport?
General truths and facts: Poland is in the European Union.

content.subtitle: Emphatic form
content.description: We can also use do/does in present simple statements for emphasis.
examples: You don't like going by bus, do you? Actually, I do like going by bus for short distances.
The bus isn't quicker than the train but it does stop right outside the factory.

content.subtitle: Form
items: statement: I am driving... You/we/they are driving... He/she/it is driving...
negative: I'm not driving... You/we/they aren't driving... He/she/it isn't driving...
question: Am I driving...? Are you/we/they driving...? Is he/she/it driving...?

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions happening now: Mike is driving to work at the moment.
Temporary series of actions: Taxi drivers aren't stopping at the train station because of the roadworks.
Temporary situations: Are they staying in a hotel near the Olympic stadium?
Changing and developing situations: Holidays abroad are becoming increasingly popular.
Annoying habits (usually with always): Dad is always cleaning the car when I want to use it!

content.subtitle: Form
items: have/has + past participle
statement: I/you/we/they have flown... He/she/it has flown...
negative: I/you/we/they haven't flown... He/she/it hasn't flown...
question: Have I/you/we/they flown...? Has he/she/it flown...?

content.subtitle: Use
items: Situations and states that started in the past and are still true: She's had her motorbike for over six years.
A series of actions continuing up to now: We've travelled by taxi, bus, plane and train – all in the last twenty-four hours!
Completed actions at a time in the past which is not mentioned: Have you ever flown in a helicopter?
Completed actions where the important thing is the present result: I've booked the coach tickets.

content.subtitle: Notes
items: Phrases such as It's the first/second/etc time... are followed by the present perfect simple: It's the second time I've been on a plane.

Speakers of American English often use the past simple in situations where speakers of British English would use the present perfect simple. US: We already saw the Sphinx. UK: We've already seen the Sphinx.

Speakers of American English use gotten as the past participle of the verb get, except when get means have or possess. US: We've already gotten Dan a new backpack. UK: We've already got Dan a new rucksack.

content.subtitle: Form
items: statement: I/you/we/they have been travelling... He/she/it has been travelling...
negative: I/you/we/they haven't been travelling... He/she/it hasn't been travelling...
question: Have I/you/we/they been travelling...? Has he/she/it been travelling...?

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions continuing up to the present moment: We have been driving for hours. Can't we have a break soon?
Actions stopping just before the present moment: I'm out of breath because I've been running to get here in time.

content.subtitle: Notes
items: The present perfect continuous is often used with words and phrases like all day/week/year/etc, for, since, just, etc.: We've been walking for hours and I need a rest.
The present perfect continuous is not normally used with the words ever and never. ✓ Have you ever flown in a helicopter before?

Sometimes there is very little difference in meaning between the present perfect simple and the present perfect continuous and sometimes there is a difference in meaning. I have worked at the airport for four years. (focus on duration) I have been working at the airport for four years. (focus on activity). I have read that book about cruise ships. (finished) I have been reading that book about cruise ships. (not finished).

content.subtitle: Use
content.description: Stative verbs are not normally used in continuous tenses because they don't describe actions.
examples: I see what you mean. (not I am seeing what you mean.)

categories.name: Thinking
categories.verbs: believe, imagine, know, mean, think, understand

categories.name: Emotions
categories.verbs: hate, like, love, need, prefer, satisfy, want

categories.name: Possession and relationships between things
categories.verbs: belong to, consist of, have, include, involve, own

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: Some verbs (such as be, have, imagine, look, see, smell, taste, think) are stative with one meaning and non-stative with another meaning.
examples: Do you have your plane ticket with you? (state: possession)
Are you having lunch at the moment? (action: eating)

sections.title: A: Present simple or continuous?
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Elizabeth usually goes / is usually going to bed at around eleven o'clock.
Dan talks / is talking on the other phone right now.
We don't eat / aren't eating any meat at the moment as we're both on a diet.
Does air travel get / Is air travel getting increasingly safe?
My mum calls / is calling me every weekend without fail.
How much do babysitters generally earn / are babysitters generally earning?
You always come / You're always coming up with excuses for not having done your homework. It's so annoying!
I don't go / I'm not going out much during the week but I always try / I'm always trying to go out somewhere on Saturday night.
No, the train does stop / is stopping at Cirencester on Saturdays.
My mum takes / is taking part in ice-skating competitions almost every weekend.

sections.title: B: Error correction
sections.instructions: Rewrite correctly. Change the words or phrases in bold.

items.sentence: My dad is often getting up late on Saturday mornings.
Are you speaking any other languages apart from English?
I already buy all my Christmas presents and it's only October!
It's the first time I'm ever having a party at home.
Actually, I think Darren does works quite hard sometimes.
Carlo is never eating Chinese food before.
Sean already books a table for tonight.
Needs Melanie any help painting her new flat?

sections.title: C: Present perfect simple & continuous
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: It's the first time (I / ever / eat) octopus!
Sandy (not / see) his sister since she went to university.
They (go) on holiday to Spain and won't be back until the end of the month.
Poor Tracy! She (write) that essay for hours now and she still hasn't finished!
(you / ever / meet) anyone famous?
(I / try) to get in touch with Jenny all morning but I can't find her anywhere.
(I / not finish) the book yet so I can't tell you what happens.
(you / already / decide) where you're going this summer?
(We / live) here for the last six years.
(I / never / hear) such nonsense!

sections.title: D: Adverbs of time
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Have you dreamt of winning the lottery? ... ever
I haven't worked out how to set the timer on the video ... .
My dad's lived in the same house ... he was born.
The film's only been on a couple of minutes. ...
Bruce has knocked three men out of the competition ... far.
I ... get the chance to get any exercise - I'm just too busy.
He's only ... got home.
It's eleven o'clock and Todd hasn't come home. ... Where could he be?
I've never met Ruth ... . What's she like?
Have you finished ... ? That was quick!
Ian ...... a shower at the moment, so could you call back in about half an hour?
options: A takes
B is taking
C has taken
D has been taking

B already writing
C have already written
D have already been writing

options: A Do you watch
B Are you watching
C Watched you

options: A do you usually play
B are you usually playing
C have you usually played
D have you usually been playing

items.sentence: That's the first time ...... an answer right today!
options: A I get
B I am getting
C I have got
D I have been getting

items.sentence: Jessica ...... has left, I'm afraid.
options: A already
B yet
C still
D so far

items.sentence: Dan ...... in the living room while we redecorate his bedroom.
options: A sleeps
B is sleeping

D does sleep

items.sentence: Unfortunately, Simone ...... a day off very often.
options: A doesn't get
B isn't getting
C hasn't got
D hasn't been getting

items.sentence: Actually, I ...... a cup of tea first thing every morning but then I switch to coffee.
options: A do drink
B am drinking
C have drunk
D have been drinking

sections.title: H: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Find the extra word in each line.

items.sentence: I've been to travelling round Europe all summer.
It's the first time I've ever been going abroad, and I've had a fantastic time!
I've seen loads of interesting places and I have to also made loads of new friends.
I've been decided to stay in touch with them now I'm back.
One of them, Giselle, is French. She was making on holiday too.
We now send text messages are to each other all the time.
They're usually in English because my French isn't very good! I'm planning to have visit her in France next year sometime.
I hope I can. I am love meeting people from other countries!
I want to have had lots of friends from all over the world!
Travelling certainly broadens the mind but it also is broadens your circle of friends!

sections.title: I: Cloze
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: You've (1) looking at that timetable for the last ten minutes.
1 (2) wish you'd be quiet!
I've (3) a splitting headache thanks to you!
You're both (4) very silly.
(5) is no point at all in blaming each other.
You (6) quite right, darling.
I (7) sorry,' said Sheila.
Well, it (8) like we (9) definitely missed the last train today.
1 (10) so,' said Sheila.
Well, one thing is (11) in doubt,' said Matt.
This is the worst holiday we've (12) been on,' said Matt.

sections.title: J: Key word transformation
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
What's the price of the tickets, Jimmy? (much)
How ...... Jimmy?
items.answer: much do the tickets cost / much are the tickets

items.sentence:
Are these your trainers? (to)
Do you ......?

Sasha's not keen on team sports at all. (like)
Sasha ...... team sports at all.

It's only her second time in a recording studio. (been)
She ...... in a recording studio once before.

We got here three hours ago. (have)
We ...... three hours.

I started writing this hours ago and it's still not right. (writing)
I ...... hours and it's still not right.

This is my first experience of flying alone. (time)
It is the first ...... alone.

Sharon's in the bath at the moment. (a)
Sharon ...... at the moment.

Paul enjoys surprises apart from on his birthday. (does)
Paul ...... surprises, just not on his birthday!
